title: Check Mates

jokes.text: Oscar and his son both confess they knew they weren't being chased, but each pretended for the other's sake
jokes.setup: Oscar confesses he lied about being a fugitive to spend time with his son
jokes.punchline: His son reveals he also knew — they were both pretending to enjoy a fake adventure together
jokes.explanation: A sweet, structurally clean mutual-confession reveal. Both characters simultaneously abandon a shared pretense, which is an elegant comedic mirror. Character-specific and earns its sentiment without being sappy. The double-confession structure is well-executed but not surprising enough to score higher.

jokes.text: Oscar lists the activity options for his son while he's inside: 'lay down inside, get scared of the dark inside, or play hide-and-go-seek inside'
jokes.setup: Oscar tells his son to pick what to do when he gets back, presenting it like exciting choices
jokes.punchline: The 'choices' are all pathetically limited indoor activities — essentially nothing, nothing, and nothing — delivered as if they're legitimate options
jokes.explanation: Oscar's eccentric, limited world is perfectly encapsulated in these options. The comedy comes from the fact that he genuinely thinks these are reasonable alternatives. Good character integration — this is distinctly Oscar-flavored humor — but not a huge laugh.

jokes.text: Oscar delivers an ultimatum: 'I don't like the smell of marijuana or your behavior when I've caught you, so it's me or the weed.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is warmly bonding with his son in a sweet father-son moment
jokes.punchline: He ends the scene by demanding his son choose between him and marijuana — a strikingly banal but revealing ultimatum that undercuts the sentimentality
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Oscar — a man who spent much of the series as a pot-growing hippie fugitive is now issuing anti-weed ultimatums. The irony is rich but understated. The abruptness of the delivery after the warmth of the scene lands well. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Oscar immediately runs outside yelling 'Hello!' as soon as he signs out
jokes.setup: Oscar said 'this shouldn't take too long' before going in to sign out
jokes.punchline: He bursts out immediately with just a cheerful 'Hello!' — implying either he's thrilled to be free or the whole thing was comically instant
jokes.explanation: A small, quick beat. The comedy is in the abruptness — he's barely gone before he's back. Functional but minor.

jokes.text: Oscar immediately goes back outside chanting 'Outside, outside, outside, outside, outside...'
jokes.setup: Oscar just asked his son to wait patiently inside
jokes.punchline: The moment he's free, Oscar manically chants 'outside' like a person escaping imprisonment — which he essentially was
jokes.explanation: The repetition sells it. Oscar spent years either in prison or on the run; the visceral joy of being outdoors expressed through manic repetition is a great character beat. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George and Lucille were finally having dinner with their wall investors, and they were waiting for the right opportunity to lie about why they couldn't build it.'
jokes.setup: A dinner scene is set up as a business meeting
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly states they're strategizing their deception — framing it as casually as if they were waiting for dessert
jokes.explanation: The AD narrator's deadpan omniscient voice is a key comedic tool, and this is a clean example. The word 'opportunity' framing deception as an occasion to seize is perfectly Bluth.

jokes.text: 'The least we can do is pick up the check. You have burned so many phones talking to us.'
jokes.setup: George and Lucille are hosting a nice dinner and offering to pay as a gesture of hospitality
jokes.punchline: They offhandedly reference the illegal burner phone usage as if it's a normal conversational courtesy
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy — the casualness of referencing criminal behavior as a social grace is very Bluth. Solid but not remarkable.

jokes.text: George and Lucille try to use Lindsay losing the election as their excuse not to build the wall, only to be told Sally Sitwell publicly supports the wall
jokes.setup: The Bluths have a rehearsed story: Lindsay lost, her opponent is anti-wall, their hands are tied
jokes.punchline: The investors immediately counter that Sitwell is publicly PRO-wall, dismantling the entire prepared lie in one sentence
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed escalating reversal. The Bluths' plan is elaborately stupid, and the investors' deadpan knowledge cut immediately to the core. The rhythm of the setup (building, building) versus the instant deflation works beautifully. Rewatch value because you can see the Bluths squirming in retrospect.

jokes.text: 'We did not expect you to know that.'
jokes.setup: The investors have just revealed they know the truth about Sitwell's wall support, exposing the Bluths' lie
jokes.punchline: Rather than deny it, George or Lucille flatly admits they didn't expect the investors to be informed — essentially confessing their contempt for the investors
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional one-liner. It's four words that perfectly encapsulate Bluth arrogance — rather than pretend it was a misunderstanding, they accidentally reveal they had no respect for the investors' intelligence. The economy is near-perfect. It's both a confession and an insult delivered without awareness. Strongly quotable and character-specific to the Bluths' oblivious condescension.

jokes.text: Narrator: '...George Sr. had no choice but to tell a lie with the word 'truth' in it.'
jokes.setup: George is caught in a lie and says 'Look, we invested your money. That's the truth.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately undercuts it by noting he had no choice but to 'tell a lie with the word truth in it' — a precise, elegant observation about George's verbal sleight of hand
jokes.explanation: This is the AD narrator at its best — a meta-observation that's simultaneously a character insight and a perfectly-worded joke. 'A lie with the word truth in it' is the exact formulation of how this con man operates. It's original, economical, and deeply character-integrated. One of the stronger jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Well, but, the banks are closed, you know, tomorrow being the seventh.'
jokes.setup: The investors push back and demand their money back
jokes.punchline: George produces a hilariously specific but obviously fabricated excuse — 'tomorrow being the seventh' — as if bank holidays follow no known calendar
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'the seventh' is the joke — it sounds like something a con man reaches for in desperation. Good but not great.

jokes.text: George confesses the truth — beginning with 'which is what my son insisted we do from the start' and then pivoting to: 'The doctors give him three months to...'
jokes.setup: George says he wants to tell the truth, which his son insisted on — a seemingly genuine moment
jokes.punchline: He immediately starts to deploy a fake dying-son sympathy play before abandoning it mid-sentence and actually confessing
jokes.explanation: An excellent character-comedy moment. George Sr. tries to pivot into a dying-son manipulation even while claiming to tell the truth, then stops himself. This is George Sr. in a nutshell — incapable of honesty even when attempting it, instinctively reaching for exploitation. Strong character integration score because this could only be George Sr.

jokes.text: 'Uh, not the regular ones; the Mexican Romneys.'
jokes.setup: George explains they lost money trying to get their land back and 'then the Romneys came'
jokes.punchline: 'The Mexican Romneys' — an absurd reference to Mitt Romney's family's Mexican heritage, delivered as a throwaway clarification
jokes.explanation: A dense, quick-fire reference joke that rewards knowledge. The show's writers squeeze in the fact that Mitt Romney's family has deep Mexican roots as a throwaway clarification — 'not the regular ones.' The word economy is outstanding. It's unexpected and rewards attentive viewers. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: 'And my wife is leaving me, and I don't know what to do. And I think she's a surfer, and I can't get hard.'
jokes.setup: George has been confessing financial failures; the investors declare 'that is more than enough honesty'
jokes.punchline: George barrels past their signal to stop, adding personal/sexual confessions that the investors explicitly don't want — escalating to impotence
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is sharp — they've signaled stop, and he goes further into intimate oversharing. 'I think she's a surfer' is a great absurd detail (it's not the obvious follow-up to 'she's leaving me'), and ending on impotence is a solid punchline. Very George Sr.

jokes.text: 'That is more than enough honesty.'
jokes.setup: George has just overshared about his wife possibly being a surfer and his impotence
jokes.punchline: The investors' flat, dignified recoil is the punchline — the phrase 'more than enough honesty' is both a complaint and an ironic inversion (honesty is usually considered a virtue)
jokes.explanation: A tight reactive punchline. 'More than enough honesty' treats honesty as a commodity that can be oversupplied — a funny inversion. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Honesty unexpectedly rewards George and Lucille with a $3 million 'monetary gift for removing shame'
jokes.setup: George has humiliated himself with a stream of confessions the investors found uncomfortable
jokes.punchline: The investors offer $3 million specifically to give the Bluths the 'opportunity to experience dignity' — their cringing oversharing is rewarded
jokes.explanation: A brilliant structural irony — the show's thesis that honesty pays is made literal and absurd. The Bluths' shameless oversharing is treated as a windfall. This also sets up the later disaster when Michael spends the money, giving it strong episode-level rewatch value.

jokes.text: 'I mean, there would still be shame. Yeah, I mean, we had shame walking in the door, but, I mean, wow...'
jokes.setup: The investors offer $3 million to remove shame from the Bluths
jokes.punchline: George and Lucille openly acknowledge they arrived with shame and that $3 million won't fully remove it — while simultaneously being excited about the money
jokes.explanation: A nice small grace note — the Bluths cheerfully acknowledge their permanent shame while negotiating its cash value. Good character work.

jokes.text: The Bluths immediately have an offshore Cayman Islands account ready for the wire transfer — 'She's got that.'
jokes.setup: The investors ask if there's a bank account that 'would avoid unwanted attention'
jokes.punchline: Without missing a beat, Lucille confirms she has exactly such an account — the Bluths' criminal infrastructure is always at the ready
jokes.explanation: The immediate, casual readiness is the joke — no hesitation, no shame, no explanation needed. 'She's got that' is beautifully terse. Rewatch value because this account becomes the engine of the entire episode's plot.

jokes.text: Michael 'got off on his son's floor, which he claimed was a force of habit'
jokes.setup: Michael is going to visit Fakeblock employees
jokes.punchline: The narrator's phrasing ('got off on his son's floor, which he claimed was a force of habit') implies this is a recurring and suspicious behavior Michael has a ready excuse for
jokes.explanation: A small narrator joke about Michael's unhealthy attachment to George Michael. Functional but minor.

jokes.text: The Fakeblock employees are playing games instead of working because 'the new president thinks it's good for morale' — but 'we'd rather get paid'
jokes.setup: Michael discovers employees playing games during work hours
jokes.punchline: The explanation is that the president mandated morale activities — but the employees simply note they'd prefer being paid, cutting through the corporate nonsense
jokes.explanation: A solid observational beat about tech company culture — the morale activities vs. actual pay trade-off is relatable. Not exceptional but functional.

jokes.text: 'The new president gave you checks. You just can't, uh, cash 'em.'
jokes.setup: Employees complain they'd rather get paid than play games
jokes.punchline: George Michael defends the president by noting they DID receive checks — just uncashable ones — which is technically true and completely useless
jokes.explanation: A sharp piece of logic-chopping comedy. The distinction between 'receiving checks' and 'being able to cash them' is functionally worthless but delivered with total earnestness. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gob's presidential catchphrase: 'Did somebody wonder... if somebody else made Gob the president? I wonder.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks 'Please don't tell me you made Gob the president' — anticipating Gob's entrance
jokes.punchline: Gob enters with his catchphrase, but it requires three sentences to deliver what should be a two-word intro — the narrator even notes 'Catchphrase could use some pruning'
jokes.explanation: A meta-comedic moment that works on multiple levels: Gob enters with his signature catchphrase, the narrator immediately roasts its wordiness, and the catchphrase itself is demonstrably unwieldy. The character integration is near-perfect — Gob's oblivious grandiosity is perfectly encapsulated. The self-awareness of 'Catchphrase could use some pruning' is outstanding AD narrator work.

jokes.text: Employee: 'He's a singer, I think. Like, a gay one. And, you know, you can't convert that away, but yeah...' about George Michael (the singer)
jokes.setup: Michael says 'Can I talk to George Michael?' and an employee doesn't know who that is
jokes.punchline: The employee assumes Michael means George Michael the pop singer, notes he's gay, and adds that 'you can't convert that away' — an inadvertent reference to Gob's conversion therapy subplot
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly layered joke: the George Michael name confusion (the show's oldest recurring gag) gets a new dimension as the employee talks about George Michael the singer being gay, which unwittingly references the Gob conversion therapy storyline running through Season 5. Two simultaneous callbacks made to look like simple name-confusion. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: 'Once you're gay, you're gay, Michael! God, Michael's so stupid. Everybody's gonna see.'
jokes.setup: Michael has corrected the employee about the name 'George Michael vs. George Maharis'
jokes.punchline: Gob shouts this as Michael leaves — ostensibly about the name confusion but clearly about Gob's own gay panic subplot, delivered as if it's Michael's problem
jokes.explanation: A superb piece of comedy that works through misdirection and character psychology. Gob's outburst ostensibly addresses the Michael/George Michael confusion, but it's transparently about his own repressed sexuality. The projection onto Michael ('everybody's gonna see') is perfectly Gob. High quotability and strong character integration — this could only come from Gob in this context.

jokes.text: 'No, I keep getting Catwoman.' (in response to 'Can't you get an intern?')
jokes.setup: Michael suggests George Michael get an intern to help manage expenses
jokes.punchline: George Michael says he 'keeps getting Catwoman' — a throwaway absurd line that implies his intern search results keep producing someone in a Catwoman costume, with zero explanation
jokes.explanation: A perfect non-sequitur drop. The specificity of 'Catwoman' with zero explanation or follow-up is the entire joke. It's the kind of throwaway AD line that rewards rewatchers who try to decode it. Extraordinary economy — four words that imply an entire absurd off-screen situation. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'It's the thread-count question. It's gotta be the thread-count question.'
jokes.setup: Michael says 'What's that?' as George Michael's phone buzzes
jokes.punchline: George Michael pre-emptively assumes it's 'the thread-count question' — a seemingly specific bureaucratic anxiety that is never explained, delivered with tired certainty
jokes.explanation: A small absurd beat. The specificity of 'the thread-count question' is funny in isolation — it implies a whole internal culture of tedious Fakeblock bureaucracy — but it doesn't land with full impact because it's never explained. Good economy, moderate payoff.

jokes.text: The paycheck from the banana stand turns out to be made out to 'George Michael' as a 'topping' — 'Payment of two drums of dried George Michael. A cocoa-based, non-digestive colorant.'
jokes.setup: Michael presents George Michael's 'first paycheck' from the banana stand as a sentimental gift
jokes.punchline: He reveals George Michael wasn't legally paid — he was classified as a topping, 'two drums of dried George Michael,' a cocoa-based colorant
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. It operates on multiple levels: it's a dark joke about child labor evasion, it's absurdist (classifying a child as an ingredient), and the specific language — 'two drums of dried George Michael, a cocoa-based, non-digestive colorant' — is extraordinary in its bureaucratic precision. The callback to the banana stand (itself a multi-season running gag) gives it enormous weight. 'I used to love those. I still do, kiddo' is a perfect button that's both warm and deeply disturbing. This approaches 9.5-level territory for AD specifically.

jokes.text: 'There's always money in the banana stand.' / 'Right. Where is that from? Is that from something?'
jokes.setup: Michael says the iconic Season 1 line 'there's always money in the banana stand'
jokes.punchline: George Michael asks if it's 'from something' — a meta-joke that the line has become so embedded in the show's mythology that even characters within the show treat it as a cultural reference
jokes.explanation: A brilliant meta-callback. The line is one of the most famous in the entire series, and having George Michael (who missed the original delivery) ask if it's 'from something' is a perfect piece of self-aware comedy. For longtime fans, this is a high-impact moment of recognition. The meta layer — the show acknowledging its own classic line — is rare and well-executed. High quotability for the fanbase.

jokes.text: George Michael notices the banana stand income never appears in the books — which leads to discovering the Cayman Islands check
jokes.setup: Michael sentimentally says 'there's always money in the banana stand' as a truism about honest work
jokes.punchline: George Michael actually did the books and found no banana stand income — exposing that the 'always money' was actually secret offshore funds
jokes.explanation: The episode's central mystery reveal and a masterful structural piece. The iconic 'there's always money in the banana stand' line — used sentimentally throughout the episode — turns out to be literally true, but in a completely different way than anyone (in-universe or in-audience) knew. This is AD's signature layered callback writing at its best. The structural payoff from Season 1 is outstanding.

jokes.text: 'That's an island. This is a check from the Cayman Islands.'
jokes.setup: They look at the logo on the check, assume it's the bank's logo
jokes.punchline: George Michael identifies it as an island — specifically, the Cayman Islands — the reveal landing with comic understatement
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of the revelation is well-paced. 'That's an island' as a simple declarative punchline to a slow burn is satisfying. Functional but the real joke is the structural reveal (id 32), not this line alone.

jokes.text: Michael tries to reclaim the Cayman Islands check: 'But, uh, give it to me. It's not even for you. It's for toppings.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is holding the historical check from the banana stand
jokes.punchline: Michael reclaims it by pointing out it's not for George Michael — it's for 'toppings,' a callback to the child-as-ingredient joke earlier
jokes.explanation: A clean callback that rewards the earlier joke by treating 'toppings' as a genuine legal category. The fact that Michael uses this as a legitimate reason to take the check is peak Bluth self-serving logic.

jokes.text: Dusty is introduced as an expert on 'Orient... Uh... Asian inscrutability. Very good.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. introduces Dusty as an expert on Asian culture
jokes.punchline: He starts to say 'Oriental' and self-corrects mid-word to 'Asian' — a cringe-comedy beat about George Sr.'s casual racism barely concealed by a last-second correction
jokes.explanation: A classic cringe beat. The partial-word self-correction ('Orient...Uh...') is better than if he'd simply said the wrong word — the effort to correct himself while still being George Sr. is very specific and character-integrated.

jokes.text: 'I lost this face when I was 65. I'm keeping the money.'
jokes.setup: Dusty says they can 'save face' by giving the money back
jokes.punchline: George Sr. takes 'save face' literally and says he lost his face at 65 anyway — a literal misreading that doubles as a dark joke about aging and as perfect George Sr. stubbornness
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. The wordplay on 'save face' is executed with perfect economy and the punchline works on three levels simultaneously: literal (his face is aging), idiomatic (he doesn't care about dignity), and character (pure Bluth selfishness). 'I'm keeping the money' as the flat conclusion is perfect. Strong case for being among the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Hard to hear, I know. But there's freedom for you in the exchange.' / 'You said we'd get ice cream. I have to get Dusty ice cream.'
jokes.setup: Dusty delivers a wise speech about freedom and dignity
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals that 'Dusty' is a child he picked up, and the profound wisdom was delivered by someone who needs to be taken for ice cream
jokes.explanation: A brilliant structural misdirection — we've been listening to Dusty's culturally sensitive advice and treating it seriously, only to discover the source is a child demanding ice cream. The gap between the profundity of the advice and the mundanity of the ice cream request is perfect. This is AD's signature recontextualization technique.

jokes.text: 'Stan, turn down Wayne Brady!' / 'I surely couldn't.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is living with an elderly man named Stan and asks him to turn down the TV
jokes.punchline: Stan's response 'I surely couldn't' is either an old man's stubbornness or, since it's Wayne Brady playing himself, an in-joke about a performer refusing to mute his own show
jokes.explanation: A quick joke that lands primarily because Wayne Brady playing himself adds a meta layer. 'I surely couldn't' is pleasingly old-fashioned and could mean multiple things. Efficient.

jokes.text: 'I just wanted to get out of doing things like Straightbait. I just couldn't do another group scene, but I love staying at Tent City.'
jokes.setup: DeBrie/Mark Cherry explain how they ended up homeless at Maeby's retirement community
jokes.punchline: The explanation involves fleeing the low-budget adult film 'Straightbait' and then squatting in what turned out to be a camping supply store running a summer sale
jokes.explanation: The reveal that 'Tent City' was a camping store running a sale is a clean misdirection. 'Straightbait' as a title callback adds context. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Yes, but that turned out to be a travel and adventure store. And we were squatting right in the middle of their summer two-for-one.'
jokes.setup: They say they 'love staying at Tent City'
jokes.punchline: It was not a tent city at all — it was a camping equipment store, and they were squatting during a sale
jokes.explanation: The misdirection on 'Tent City' is clean. The detail of 'their summer two-for-one' gives it specificity that elevates it above a generic reveal. Good structure.

jokes.text: Mark Cherry says to Maeby: 'You're rather quite attractive for an older lady.' Maeby responds: 'That is your half-sister.'
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry is considering whether Maeby (disguised as an old lady) 'has a half-daughter' he could date
jokes.punchline: He pivots to complimenting Maeby herself, only to be told she's his half-sister — a double layer of inappropriate attraction that is pure AD
jokes.explanation: This is peak AD incest-adjacent comedy. The show has made near-incest a recurring comic motif, and this escalates it naturally — first asking about a half-daughter (already sketchy) then shifting to hitting on the disguised woman herself. The flat, immediate 'That is your half-sister' as a punchline is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'Okay, the party. 'Cause I was gonna say, does she have, like, a half-daughter?'
jokes.setup: Mark Cherry is told Maeby is his half-sister — he takes a beat
jokes.punchline: He pivots to asking about a 'half-daughter' — continuing to try to find a family member to pursue romantically, unfazed by the half-sister revelation
jokes.explanation: A solid escalation beat — he doesn't recoil from finding out Maeby is his half-sister; he immediately looks for the next viable family option. The unfazed pivot is characteristic of AD's treatment of inappropriate attraction as mundane.

jokes.text: Maeby is cornered by her boyfriend Stan asking if these are her family — and she admits yes
jokes.setup: Maeby has been successfully hiding her family and her real age from Stan
jokes.punchline: Caught with nowhere to hide, she simply admits 'Yes. They are.' — giving up her entire elaborate con in one sentence
jokes.explanation: A cringe beat that works through defeat. Maeby's willingness to immediately abandon years of deception when caught is true to character. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Stan responds to meeting Maeby's family by saying 'I shall pack my things' — and apparently leaves immediately
jokes.setup: Maeby finally comes clean about her family
jokes.punchline: Stan's response — not anger, not questions, just the formal 'I shall pack my things' — suggests one look at this family was enough to end the relationship
jokes.explanation: The formal register of 'I shall pack my things' is perfect — Stan doesn't argue, protest, or ask questions. One glance at the assembled Funkes/Bluths and he just... leaves. The dignity of his departure is funnier than any argument would be. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: 'Well, that was a freebie. Now I just gotta figure out how to get rid of you guys.'
jokes.setup: Stan just walked out; Maeby's family solved one of her problems
jokes.punchline: Maeby immediately reframes the family's arrival as only solving the Stan problem — she still wants rid of them, undercutting any warm moment
jokes.explanation: A perfect Maeby moment — she treats a potential emotional reunion as a transaction and immediately categorizes her family as the next problem to solve. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: DeBrie forces a group hug: 'Hug. Hug. Hug, DeBrie. Group hug.'
jokes.setup: Maeby just said she wants to get rid of her family
jokes.punchline: DeBrie immediately insists on a group hug, chanting it with increasing insistence while clearly no one else wants one
jokes.explanation: A minor cringe beat. The repetition is slightly funny but the joke doesn't build to much. DeBrie forcing uncomfortable warmth is character-appropriate but the execution is thin.

jokes.text: Michael narrates to himself: 'Where are you, banana stand money?'
jokes.setup: Michael is investigating the Cayman Islands account
jokes.punchline: He narrates his own investigation like a private detective in a film noir, talking to money as if it can answer — a small absurd moment
jokes.explanation: A minor beat. Personifying the money is mildly funny but doesn't rise above functional. The payoff of the investigation is elsewhere.

jokes.text: Michael finds the 'there's always money in the banana stand' inscription — then concludes: 'That's gotta be from South Park.'
jokes.setup: Michael finds the iconic line written somewhere and stares at it
jokes.punchline: He concludes it must be from South Park — failing to recognize one of the most famous lines from his own family's history
jokes.explanation: An exceptional meta-comedy moment. This is a callback to the earlier in-episode George Michael question ('Is that from something?') and to the Season 1 iconic line. Michael's failure to recognize one of the defining lessons of his own childhood — and attributing it to South Park — is a perfect joke about how the Bluth children were never actually given wisdom, just subjected to a simulation of it. High quotability for fans.

jokes.text: Gob's 'motivational speech' to employees: describing a 'knot in my stomach' as 'a little ball of energy' that 'keeps me frightened when I should be calm' and 'pumps adrenaline when ordinary blood is what I need'
jokes.setup: Gob is supposed to give an inspirational speech to the tech employees
jokes.punchline: His 'inspirational' reframe of anxiety is actually just a longer description of anxiety — he never actually turns the negative into a positive, just redescribes the symptoms with enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Gob's speech is a prolonged piece of comic obliviousness — he frames anxiety symptoms (fright, adrenaline, nausea) as positive without ever explaining how they help. 'It pumps adrenaline through my veins when ordinary blood is what I need' is a genuinely great line that sounds inspirational while describing a medical problem. Strong character integration — this is exactly how Gob operates. One of the better sustained comedy sequences in the episode.

jokes.text: 'People say you shouldn't start a company without cracking the technology that supports the flagship product of the company. For obvious reasons. Well, why not?'
jokes.setup: Gob is listing examples of 'why not' thinking applied to conventional wisdom
jokes.punchline: He applies 'why not' to the specific absurdity of Fakeblock — a company built entirely on fake software — delivering it with the same inspirational energy as the other examples
jokes.explanation: The peak of Gob's speech and one of the sharpest jokes in the episode. The joke requires the audience to hold the knowledge that Fakeblock is completely fake software, then watch Gob encourage his employees to ignore 'obvious reasons' for why this is a problem — with 'for obvious reasons' as the only break in his inspiration. The 'why not' reframes a catastrophic fraud as entrepreneurial boldness. Excellent structure and character integration.

jokes.text: 'And what if you find out you're never gonna get paid, there is no product, and you're like, Well, we can't let him get away with this. Well, why not? That may have been a step too far.'
jokes.setup: Gob has been rallying the employees with 'why not' rhetoric
jokes.punchline: He accidentally applies 'why not' to the employees' potential legal recourse against him for fraud — then immediately self-corrects with 'that may have been a step too far,' acknowledging he talked himself into something dangerous
jokes.explanation: The best moment of Gob's speech and one of the top jokes in the episode. Gob's rhetorical momentum carries him into accidentally encouraging employee revolt against himself, then his sudden awareness ('that may have been a step too far') is perfectly timed. The structure is airtight: the 'why not' game logics its way to Gob's own destruction, and his recognition comes just a beat too late to be useful. Near-perfect character integration — this is exactly how Gob's intelligence and stupidity coexist.

jokes.text: Maeby announces she sold Fakeblock for $3 million — exactly the same amount as the Chinese investment money
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are celebrating selling Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: The sale price is $3 million — the exact amount of the Chinese investors' money that Michael just discovered is missing, setting up a chain of disaster that becomes clear only later
jokes.explanation: A structural joke that requires full episode context to appreciate. The amount $3 million appears three times in this episode (Chinese investors' money, Fakeblock sale, Michael's purchase) and each instance compounds the disaster. The audience may not connect it immediately, making this high-rewatch material. The structural precision is exceptional AD writing.

jokes.text: 'I feel bad for the new owner. It's bogus software. I mean, what if it bankrupts them?' / 'Oh, click. How sorry can you feel for some guy who's obviously incredibly rich and has $3 million to drop on a .wav file that's just the sound of a piece of wood.'
jokes.setup: George Michael expresses guilt about selling fake software
jokes.punchline: Maeby dismisses the moral concern — the buyer is clearly rich, and the product is literally 'a .wav file that's just the sound of a piece of wood.' Her click of dismissal sets up the reveal that the buyer is Michael.
jokes.explanation: Works as a standalone joke and as a structural setup. 'A .wav file that's just the sound of a piece of wood' is a brilliant, specific description that confirms exactly how minimal Fakeblock is. Maeby's dismissal of George Michael's moral concerns with a 'click' — as if deleting a file — is characterful. The dramatic irony (they're laughing about a buyer who turns out to be George Michael's father) earns enormous rewatch points.

jokes.text: Gob continues his speech: 'We had those checks issued to you, and you're thinking, We want to cash those checks. Well, why not? Why the hell not? Cash those checks.'
jokes.setup: Gob has been using 'why not' to inspire employees; the checks are uncashable
jokes.punchline: He applies the 'why not' logic to cashing the uncashable checks — essentially accidentally encouraging employees to commit check fraud on uncovered accounts, while thinking he's being inspirational
jokes.explanation: A solid callback that connects two threads — the uncashable checks and the 'why not' speech. Gob encouraging employees to commit fraud while thinking he's being inspirational is good character comedy. The employees' enthusiastic response ('We've got jobs!') makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Michael on his mother's sudden warmth: 'That's what's off.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says 'I'm just happy to see you' with genuine warmth
jokes.punchline: Michael, so conditioned to Lucille's coldness, immediately identifies her warmth as suspicious — 'That's what's off' — as if happiness is the aberrant behavior
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly encapsulate Michael's entire relationship with his mother. His suspicion of her warmth is funnier than anything explicit could be — it tells us everything about Lucille's baseline behavior. Exceptional economy and character integration.

jokes.text: 'The truth came out of my mouth as easy as any lie I could've thought of. She was so believable. I thought she was lying. It was like the opposite of Pinocchio. I swear, I think my nose got smaller.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. marvels at how naturally honesty came to Lucille
jokes.punchline: He says it was 'like the opposite of Pinocchio' and that his nose 'got smaller' — a reverse-Pinocchio theory of truthfulness that is pure comic Bluth logic
jokes.explanation: The reverse-Pinocchio is a genuinely original comedic concept executed well. 'I swear, I think my nose got smaller' has the right combination of absurdist sincerity and self-congratulation. George Sr. and Lucille's self-mythologizing when they do something ordinary is well-captured.

jokes.text: 'It was like the opposite of Pinocchio. I swear, I think my nose got smaller. Well, wouldn't have been the first time.'
jokes.setup: The Pinocchio comparison — honesty making her nose shrink instead of grow
jokes.punchline: Lucille adds 'wouldn't have been the first time' — implying she's had rhinoplasty, adding another surgical vanity joke to the face-saving callback
jokes.explanation: The Pinocchio inversion is clever, and the rhinoplasty callback ties it neatly to the earlier 'face' joke. Lucille's plastic surgery as a recurring character trait makes this earned. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr. claims he 'dug a little bit deeper and found some extra capital' — Michael asks how much; scene is interrupted before the answer lands
jokes.setup: George Sr. announces the family business is 'back' with 'extra capital'
jokes.punchline: The amount is withheld by the interruption — setting up the reveal that it's the same $3M the Chinese were owed, a circular disaster
jokes.explanation: Structural setup for the later reveal rather than a standalone joke. The interruption is a classic AD technique of withholding information to maximize a later punchline. Functional but not independently funny.

jokes.text: George Sr. admits he's 'dug a little bit deeper and found some extra capital' — which turns out to be the Chinese investors' face-saving money
jokes.setup: Michael is thrilled that his parents have finally reformed and are being honest; they claim to have found 'extra capital'
jokes.punchline: The capital they 'found' is the money they were specifically supposed to return to the Chinese investors — they've spent it anyway, without any awareness of the consequences Michael will discover
jokes.explanation: A structural joke that pays off later. The dramatic irony of Michael celebrating his parents' reforms while they've just committed the exact transgression they were warned against is satisfying on rewatch. In the moment, it functions as misdirection.

jokes.text: Michael anticipates George Michael's disappointment about Fakeblock — 'as George Michael prepared himself for his father's disappointment' — and then Michael erupts: 'You what? That's so great!'
jokes.setup: George Michael confesses he sold Fakeblock; narrator says he was 'prepared for his father's disappointment'
jokes.punchline: Michael's reaction is pure joy — 'That's so great!' — the exact opposite of what George Michael and the audience expected
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection with good structural support from the narrator framing. The narrator's 'prepared himself for disappointment' raises expectations for one reaction, making the reversal hit harder. Works well and the irony deepens once we discover Michael is the buyer.

jokes.text: 'Mr. Big Business, sold your company. How do you feel?' / 'To be honest, I feel incredibly relieved... We had to pay off the employees, and the bank took the rest of it, but I'm glad I'm back at zero.'
jokes.setup: Michael excitedly congratulates George Michael on selling his first business for $3 million
jokes.punchline: George Michael's actual feeling is relief at being back at zero — the $3 million immediately evaporated into debts, making the whole thing essentially a wash delivered with genuine gratitude
jokes.explanation: A good satirical comment on startup economics — $3 million comes in, goes straight out, and the founder considers zero a win. Character-specific to George Michael's beleaguered state.

jokes.text: 'Did this by himself. All that burden on your shoulders. No one to help you. A little baby Atlas.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille are praising George Michael for selling Fakeblock independently
jokes.punchline: 'A little baby Atlas' — they congratulate their grandson for holding up the world alone, while being oblivious to the fact that they are the weight he's been carrying
jokes.explanation: The 'little baby Atlas' is an inspired image — the Bluths are the world that George Michael is holding up, and they're praising him for it with no self-awareness. The irony is rich and well-earned. Strong character integration because this oblivious condescension-as-praise is quintessentially Bluth grandparenting.

jokes.text: 'Find a little extra money, put it in a Bluth.' — George Sr.'s company motto
jokes.setup: George Sr. announces he's expanding and asks what the company motto is
jokes.punchline: The 'company motto' is a completely self-serving, financially irresponsible edict — to take found money and invest it in Bluth businesses — stated as if it's a well-established corporate principle
jokes.explanation: A strong character joke. George Sr. has codified his financial recklessness into a formal motto, treating 'reinvest in ourselves regardless of circumstances' as wisdom. The specificity of 'put it in a Bluth' is funny — he's bought Fakeblock, which is his own grandson's company, completing the absurd logic loop.

jokes.text: Michael bought Fakeblock through 'B.S. Enterprises' — which stands for 'Banana Stand' but is also 'Bullshit' and 'Bluth and Son'
jokes.setup: Michael announces he bought Fakeblock through a shell company called 'B.S. Enterprises'
jokes.punchline: He explains it's 'Banana Stand' but immediately acknowledges the second meaning is 'Bullshit,' then corrects to 'Bluth and Son' — three meanings stacked on the same initials, two of which perfectly describe the company
jokes.explanation: One of the top wordplay jokes of the episode. Three simultaneous meanings for the same initials — Banana Stand (the show's mythological symbol of Bluth money), Bullshit (an accurate description of Fakeblock), and Bluth and Son (Michael's dream of working with George Michael). The fact that two of three meanings describe the venture accurately, and Michael has to self-correct from 'Bullshit,' is perfectly executed. High quotability, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: 'I bought you! I had to use a shell company because I knew you were gonna give me a discount, and I wasn't gonna have that.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is confused about who bought Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals he's the buyer — and his reason for using a shell company is that he didn't want a discount, which is a completely backwards reason for corporate secrecy
jokes.explanation: Michael's logic here is perfect and uniquely his — he's so obsessed with giving his son full value and not being perceived as playing favorites that he went to elaborate lengths to pay full price for worthless software. The inversion of normal corporate behavior (hiding to avoid paying MORE) is excellent character comedy and the punchline of the Fakeblock reveal.

jokes.text: 'I'm not gonna get involved in any of that tech stuff, even though I did spend that couple of months at the Search campus. But I'm not gonna start coding, 'cause if Fakeblock doesn't work, we lose both companies for good.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains his investment plan for Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: He casually mentions spending 'a couple of months at the Search campus' (implying Google) as evidence of tech credentials, then immediately disclaims any ability to help — while cheerfully noting that if Fakeblock fails they lose everything
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat. Michael name-drops his Google visit as credentials, then admits it gave him nothing useful, then explains the entire investment is built on sand. His calm certainty about catastrophic risk is recognizably Bluth.

jokes.text: 'You thought it was over. Look how emotional he is, huh?... Listen, that's why I stepped in. Fakeblock ain't going nowhere.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is visibly distressed by the revelation that Michael bought Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Michael reads George Michael's panic as touching emotion and congratulates himself for saving the day — completely misreading that his son is horrified, not moved
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to read George Michael's distress is a running theme executed well here. He interprets horror as gratitude, which is both funny and a sad commentary on their relationship. 'Fakeblock ain't going nowhere' as a confident declaration about fake software is a good ironic button.

jokes.text: The episode's escalating 'mildly displeased' callback — Michael says he's 'mildly displeased' echoing Dusty's warning; George Sr. says 'they will be mildly displeased'; everyone uses the phrase
jokes.setup: Dusty warned earlier that keeping the money would make the Chinese 'mildly displeased' while also leading to 'merciless revenge'
jokes.punchline: Now Michael uses the same phrase about his own feelings, the characters repeat it, and 'mildly displeased' becomes a shared euphemism for catastrophic consequences — each repetition deepening the irony
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed running gag that pays off with increasing irony. The gap between 'mildly displeased' and 'merciless revenge' was already funny in Dusty's warning; now that everyone is using the phrase in an escalating catastrophe, it becomes funnier with each repetition. Good AD-style dense callback work.

jokes.text: 'You just can't say I made a good decision. Dad, I'm mildly displeased about something. This. This is what I spent your money on. Look at him.'
jokes.setup: Michael and his parents are arguing about the Chinese money
jokes.punchline: Michael pivots from defending himself to complaining about not getting credit for parenting decisions — using 'mildly displeased' in its new ironic context while simultaneously inviting them to admire George Michael as evidence of his good choices
jokes.explanation: Michael's compulsive need for his parents' approval — even when they're in crisis — is perfectly captured here. He can't resist asking for recognition even during an argument about a financial catastrophe. Good character work.

jokes.text: 'Dad, I'm mildly displeased about something.' — Michael uses the exact phrase to his father in a completely different (minor) context
jokes.setup: The phrase 'mildly displeased' has been established as code for catastrophic retribution
jokes.punchline: Michael uses it to describe his actual mild displeasure about something minor — inverting the established meaning back to literal
jokes.explanation: Using the established catastrophic understatement for a genuine minor grievance is a tight callback that inverts the joke's meaning. The audience hears the phrase, braces for the Dusty definition, and gets normal mild displeasure instead — a perfect in-episode misdirection. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: George Michael confirms to the room: 'Fakeblock is real. It's a... It's a very weird way to say it, but, you know, Fakeblock, it's real.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks George Michael to tell his parents the company is real and the investment is sound
jokes.punchline: George Michael's confirmation — 'Fakeblock is real' — is technically true (the company exists) but the hesitation and 'it's a very weird way to say it' reveals his awareness that he's lying by omission about the software
jokes.explanation: George Michael's self-aware performance of a lie is excellent character comedy. 'It's a very weird way to say it' is a moment of genuine honesty buried inside a deception — he's telling them it's odd that they're asking this while technically confirming the existence of the company, not the software. The layers of deception and self-awareness are very well-constructed.

jokes.text: 'This is the best investment this family has made in years.' / 'I feel sick.' / 'I've felt better.'
jokes.setup: George Michael gives his tepid endorsement of Fakeblock as an investment
jokes.punchline: George Sr. and Lucille's reactions — 'I feel sick' and 'I've felt better' — are the most honest things the Bluths have said in the entire episode
jokes.explanation: A great reactive beat. The Bluth parents — who have spent the episode lying constantly — now tell the truth accidentally through their visceral discomfort. The contrast with their earlier celebration is sharp. 'I've felt better' from Lucille is perfect deadpan. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Wait till you find out who the new president is.' / 'Oh, no.' / 'Did somebody say, Wait until you find out who the president is? Yes, but then we figured it out, and your father said, Oh, no.' — Gob's revised entrance
jokes.setup: Gob's 'Did somebody say...' catchphrase is set up again — but this time, someone says the lead-in AND the response before he can enter
jokes.punchline: Gob enters having to acknowledge that his catchphrase was preemptively completed — so he has to recite the entire context including the negative response, turning his entrance into a recap
jokes.explanation: The evolution of the catchphrase gag is beautifully constructed. The first time (id 24), the narrator noted it needed pruning. Now, because the punchline was delivered before he arrived, Gob has to reconstruct his entire entrance from scratch, including the negative reaction — which makes his grand entrance even more deflating. The gag has escalated perfectly across the episode. One of the strongest running gag moments.

jokes.text: 'So we've got $3 million tied up in something Gob's in charge of?' / 'Maybe we could just hire him, like, a really good assistant.' / 'One step ahead of you.'
jokes.setup: The family realizes Gob is president of their new company
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s suggestion — 'hire him a really good assistant' — to manage Gob, followed by Lucille's immediate 'One step ahead of you' implying she already has a terrible alternative lined up
jokes.explanation: A tight exchange. 'Maybe we could hire him a really good assistant' as a solution to 'Gob runs our company' is a good joke (managing him rather than trusting him), and 'one step ahead of you' sets up the reveal of the random person she's found, adding anticipatory dread.

jokes.text: Lucille has recruited Buster as Gob's 'right-hand man' — but Buster only has a hook for a hand. Gob notes: 'That would've been better if it was his right hand.'
jokes.setup: Gob excitedly announces his 'new right-hand man' — someone he found next to a police station
jokes.punchline: It's Buster, who lost his hand. Gob acknowledges the 'right-hand man' phrasing would've worked better if Buster had lost the right hand rather than the left
jokes.explanation: A callback to one of the show's longest-running visual gags (Buster's missing hand) with a new twist. Gob's genuine disappointment that the wrong hand is missing — as if Buster should have planned better — is perfect. The 'right-hand man' idiom getting literally applied to an amputee is a sharp piece of wordplay.

jokes.text: 'But I figured, if somebody's willing to spend 3KK on my company, might be able to spread wealth on myself.'
jokes.setup: Gob is reflecting on why he brought on Buster as an assistant
jokes.punchline: '3KK' as a substitute for $3 million is pure Gob — either he doesn't know the proper terminology or is affecting a style — combined with 'spread wealth on myself' as the motive for hiring someone
jokes.explanation: '3KK' is a good Gob-ism — it's the kind of pseudo-slang he'd invent to sound cool. 'Spread wealth on myself' as a hiring motive is honest and characterful. Good character integration.

jokes.text: 'Did my hand get off on this floor?' — Gob's geo-bead/George Michael misfire immediately after Buster arrives
jokes.setup: Gob's geo-bead (his version of the catchphrase) just fired based on the floor they're on
jokes.punchline: Gob asks whether his 'hand' got off on this floor — an absurd question that conflates his catchphrase device with Buster's hook hand, delivered with complete puzzlement
jokes.explanation: A quick joke that layers two running gags (the catchphrase and Buster's hand) with mild confusion. Functional but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Oscar re-emerges: 'Well, look who finally decided to show up. Steve McQueen from The Great Escape.' / 'I take it you saw the unwanted posters?' / 'Yes, and I got the hint. No one was chasing after me.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is returning after his whole 'fugitive' arc has been revealed as fake
jokes.punchline: 'Unwanted posters' instead of 'wanted posters' — Oscar casually notes the correct term for his situation, and then confirms he understood the hint that no one was pursuing him
jokes.explanation: 'Unwanted posters' is a perfect AD wordplay — a wanted poster for someone no one wants. The callback to the opening of the episode completes Oscar's arc neatly and with a good punchline. 'Steve McQueen from The Great Escape' as Michael's greeting is also good — it's an obscure enough reference to reward attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Oscar says 'I take it you saw the unwanted posters?' — referring to the 'UNWANTED' posters that apparently signaled no one was looking for him
jokes.setup: Oscar confirms he got the hint that no one was chasing him
jokes.punchline: 'Unwanted posters' vs. 'WANTED posters' — the inversion suggests posters declaring him 'unwanted' (not a fugitive, but also perhaps unloved) rather than the criminal 'WANTED' variety
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on 'unwanted' vs. 'wanted' posters is excellent — it works as a pun AND as a character statement (Oscar is literally unwanted, not just un-pursued). The double meaning is earned and specific to Oscar's character arc. 'Oh, right. It had two meanings.' confirms the writers knew exactly what they were doing.

jokes.text: 'Yes, well, that parade created a great deal of... pain for a lot of us.' — Oscar trails off meaningfully while the show plays 'For He's a Jolly Good Fellow' (implied)
jokes.setup: Michael blames Oscar's insistence on being in the parade for the subsequent events
jokes.punchline: Oscar agrees with a vague, significant ellipsis — 'a great deal of pain for a lot of us' — referencing an event whose consequences are still rippling through the episode
jokes.explanation: A quieter callback moment. The significance of the pause is that Oscar is likely the cause of multiple disasters through the parade subplot. Functional but relies heavily on context.

jokes.text: Hot Cops arrive — Gob thinks they're a surprise he orchestrated. 'Who did this? Is this because I'm still gay? Hot Cops? On such short notice? Who did this? 'Cause the conversion didn't take?'
jokes.setup: Police arrive at the party; Gob assumes he somehow arranged for Hot Cops (male strippers) as entertainment
jokes.punchline: His questions — 'Is this because I'm still gay? 'Cause the conversion didn't take?' — reveal his ongoing gay panic while simultaneously misidentifying actual cops as entertainers
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly dense joke. Multiple layers: Gob confusing real cops with strippers (a running AD gag), Gob's unresolved gay identity, his ongoing conversion therapy subplot, and his inability to comprehend his own feelings all collide in four sentences. 'Is this because I'm still gay? 'Cause the conversion didn't take?' is remarkably specific and characterful. The character integration approaches the maximum — this could literally only be Gob.

jokes.text: Gob tries to call Hot Cops while being arrested: 'Leave a message for Hot Cops! How'd you like to make a fifth of a K?'
jokes.setup: Real police are arresting Buster while Gob believes they're strippers
jokes.punchline: Gob attempts to call the actual Hot Cops service while being arrested, offering 'a fifth of a K' ($200) — both continuing the misidentification and revealing his new wealth makes him even more out of touch
jokes.explanation: 'A fifth of a K' — $200 — is Gob's opening offer for Hot Cops, and the fact that he thinks $200 is a reasonable pitch while sitting on $3 million tells us everything about Gob's financial thinking. The Hot Cops callback is well-executed. Good character work.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But the real cops weren't out of business and were about to stumble on a big piece of evidence.' — Cut to police discovering something
jokes.setup: Gob thinks he has successfully called for Hot Cops as entertainment
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly notes that unlike Gob's model, actual police are still operating — and are discovering evidence in a different part of the building
jokes.explanation: 'The real cops weren't out of business' is a good line — it implies Gob thinks of law enforcement as a competing service to Hot Cops. Functional narrator joke with good setup for the episode's cliffhanger.

jokes.text: Cop: 'Well, now I've seen it all.' — as Gob dances with a boom box
jokes.setup: Gob has grabbed a boom box and is dancing as if at a party while police are arresting Buster around him
jokes.punchline: A cop observes Gob's behavior with the understatement of the century
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat. 'Well, now I've seen it all' is a clichéd phrase that works here because it's genuinely warranted — Gob dancing with a boom box at his brother's arrest is objectively something. Economy is excellent but the originality is limited.

jokes.text: Tag scene: Gob continues to sing well after the family realizes Buster is being arrested; on the next AD: 'Gob continues to sing'
jokes.setup: Buster has been arrested; the family is presumably devastated
jokes.punchline: The 'On the next Arrested Development' preview shows Gob just... still singing, completely unaware, well after everyone else has processed the situation
jokes.explanation: The 'On the next...' tag is a classic AD comedic device, and this is an excellent example. Gob's obliviousness is so complete that his singing simply continues into the next episode preview. The image of the entire family processing a crisis while Gob happily sings in the background is a perfect character summary. Economy and character integration are near-perfect.

jokes.text: Final tag: 'I'm gonna take the stairs.' — a character's exit line after Buster's arrest
jokes.setup: The chaos of Buster's arrest, Gob dancing, police everywhere
jokes.punchline: Someone's response to all of this is the supremely mundane decision to take the stairs — a tiny act of normalcy in complete chaos, delivered with finality
jokes.explanation: A well-timed exit joke. The decision to take the stairs as the only rational response to the insanity is a good comedic button. Economy is outstanding — five words that convey complete surrender to absurdity.

tmdb_overview: George Sr., Lucille and Maeby give honesty a try. Good news becomes bad for George-Michael. Michael remembers there's always money ... somewhere.
insight: Mid-season slump: 46 jokes land at 70.9, the season's lowest impact score.
